title: Chapter 51: The Radio
content: Three days after the siege, Vivienne Duchamps stopped sleeping.

This was, Marcus noted with the weary concern of a man who'd watched Elena do the same thing, a pattern. Put a woman in his orbit who was defined by her competence, add a crisis that required exactly that competence, and watch the self-care evaporate like morning fog over a wasteland. Elena ran triage until her hands shook. Junko patrolled until her Stage 2 metabolism started consuming muscle. And Vivienne — Vivienne sat in the radio room and refused to leave until the shortwave was *done*.

The radio room had been a supply closet before the outbreak. After Elena's triage expansion and the exodus of non-essential equipment, it had become New Eden's communications center — a six-by-eight concrete box on the second floor of the administration building, lit by a single bare bulb, furnished with a desk, a chair, and an increasingly unstable pile of salvaged electronics that Vivienne had arranged with the precision of a surgeon laying out instruments.

She'd been working on the shortwave since before the siege. The unit itself had come from her penthouse — a military-grade Yaesu FT-857D that she'd carried down forty-two flights of stairs in a fire escape while raiders shot at her, because a communications blackout was a fate worse than gunfire. The antenna was a coat-hanger array she'd soldered herself, mounted on the roof with duct tape and spite. The power supply was a car battery she'd wired into New Eden's generator with Elena's help and a prayer.

It still wasn't fully operational. The frequency filters needed calibrating. The signal-to-noise ratio was unacceptable. The antenna's impedance was — Vivienne had used a word that Marcus was fairly certain wasn't in any dictionary, but which had conveyed with startling clarity exactly how she felt about coat-hangers as structural elements.

So on the third morning after the siege, when Marcus climbed the stairs to the radio room with a plate of eggs and toast conjured specifically for the purpose of reminding a workaholic aristocrat that she was a biological organism with biological needs, he found the door open and the room transformed.

Vivienne was standing. Not sitting — standing, which was unusual enough that Marcus stopped in the doorway. The shortwave's front panel was lit for the first time, green and amber indicators glowing like a Christmas display, and the speaker was producing something that wasn't static.
It was a voice.

"The Relay, broadcasting on 14.250 MHz. If you're receiving this transmission, you are not alone. The Relay is a coordinated survivor network operating out of the Catskill Mountains, approximately two hundred miles east of the New York metropolitan area. We have medical facilities. We have agricultural capability. We have military support — remnants of the 10th Mountain Division, Fort Drum, supplemented by National Guard and civilian volunteers. Current population: four hundred and twelve. We are accepting refugees. We are organizing. We are *alive*."

The voice was male, mid-forties, with the measured cadence of someone who'd done radio broadcasts before — a professional calm that made the content of the message land harder, not softer, because the man sounded like he was reading a weather report instead of announcing the continued existence of human civilization.

Vivienne turned. Her grey eyes were bright — not with exhaustion, though that was present too, but with the fierce, almost manic light of someone who'd just opened a door they'd been told was locked forever.

"It's a repeating broadcast," she said. "Seventeen-minute cycle. I've been listening for three hours." She paused, and something shifted in her expression — the aristocratic mask slipping just enough to reveal the woman underneath, the one who'd spent twenty-three days alone in a penthouse telling herself that needing people was weakness. "Marcus. There's more."
"What do you mean, more?"

"The broadcast has a data segment. Encoded sideband transmission — military standard. I couldn't decode it with the equipment I had, so I modified a signal processor from the generator's control board, and—" She stopped herself, took a breath, and started over with the deliberate composure of someone who knew she was about to say something that would change everything. "The data segment contains a list. Names of confirmed survivors, their last known locations, and their status. It's updated every cycle. I assume The Relay is collecting reports from other survivor networks — ham operators, military units, anyone with transmitting capability."
Marcus set the plate down on the desk. The eggs were getting cold. "How many names?"

"Three hundred and forty-seven as of the last cycle." Vivienne's voice was very precise. "I started compiling a database. Most are in North America — military remnant groups, settlement clusters, that sort of thing. But there are international entries too. Places with infrastructure still standing. Places where the outbreak hit differently, or where the response was fast enough to—"

"Vivienne." Marcus put his hand on the desk. Not urgently — he wasn't there yet, didn't know what was coming — but with the steady weight of a man asking a woman to stop circling and just land the plane. "What did you find?"

Vivienne looked at him. And for the first time since he'd known her — through the penthouse siege and the siege of New Eden and every moment of aristocratic composure in between — Vivienne Duchamps looked uncertain.

"I found your name," she said quietly. "Marcus Chen, New York, confirmed alive. Someone reported you. I don't know who — there's no attribution in the data." She paused. "But you weren't the only Chen on the list."

The room shrank. The walls pressed in. The hum of the shortwave and the distant thrum of the generator faded to nothing, and Marcus heard his own heartbeat in the sudden void — or maybe that was the Bond, pulsing with a sharp spike of *something* that wasn't quite fear, wasn't quite hope, wasn't quite anything his emotional vocabulary had words for.
"What do you mean, I wasn't the only—"
"Chen, David and Mei-Ling." Vivienne's voice was barely above a whisper, as if the volume of the words might somehow unmake them. "Taipei. Confirmed alive. Requesting transport."
The floor dropped out from under him.

Not literally — the concrete was still there, solid and cold beneath his boots — but the sensation was identical, the stomach-lurching free-fall of a man who'd spent three years in a basement assuming the worst and two more weeks in the apocalypse confirming it. His parents. David Chen, who had taught him to play chess and driven him to science fairs and spoken Mandarin at the dinner table because he wanted his son to remember where they came from. Mei-Ling Chen, who had made the best potstickers in Queens, who had cried at his middle school graduation and pretended she wasn't crying, who had called him every Sunday without fail until the Sunday the phones stopped working.
His parents were alive.
His parents were *alive*.

Marcus sat down. He didn't decide to sit down — his legs simply stopped being legs and became something that could not support a human being, and the chair was there, and he was in it, and his hands were in his hair and his elbows were on his knees and he was breathing, just breathing, the kind of breathing that precedes either tears or vomiting and he wasn't sure which one was coming.

He'd assumed they were dead. He'd *assumed* — because the outbreak had hit hard, because Taipei was dense, because international communication had collapsed on day three, because three years in a basement had taught him that hoping for something and losing it was worse than assuming it was gone. He'd grieved them. Not properly, not the way they deserved, but in the dull, persistent way you grieve everything in the apocalypse — by pushing it down and keeping moving and telling yourself that everyone had lost someone and your pain wasn't special.
And now they were alive. In Taipei. Requesting transport.
The Bond flared.

It wasn't a word or an image or even a feeling, exactly — it was Sera, arriving, her presence rushing through the connection between them like water through a broken dam. She was in the radio room before Marcus had finished processing the first wave, her body moving through the building with the supernatural speed of a Stage 4 predator whose partner was in distress, and then she was there, real and present and cool-skinned and silver-eyed and *there*.
Her hand landed on his shoulder. Not the Shadow Weave — just her hand, five fingers, the deliberate gentleness of a creature who could tear doors off hinges choosing to be soft.

"Marcus." Her voice was low, steady, the voice she used when the world was on fire and she needed him to hear her through the flames. Through the Bond, she didn't offer empty comfort — she offered *presence*, the absolute certainty that she was with him, that he was not alone with this, that whatever came next would come to both of them.

He looked up at her. His eyes were wet. He hadn't cried since the first week — since the phones went dead and he'd sat on the basement floor and sobbed until his throat was raw — and he hadn't cried since, because there hadn't been time, because there were always more pressing crises, because crying in the apocalypse was a luxury you couldn't afford.
"My parents," he said. "They're alive, Sera. They're in Taipei. They're—"

He couldn't finish. The tears came, and he let them, and Sera's hand moved from his shoulder to the back of his neck, cool fingers pressing into the tight muscles, the touch transmitting through the Bond as clearly as language: *I know. I'm here. We'll fix this.*

"We'll find them," she said. Out loud, not through the Bond — because some things needed to be spoken, needed to exist in the air and not just in the space between minds. "We'll find them, Marcus. I promise you."
Lily found them an hour later.

Marcus had moved from the chair to the floor — not because he was in worse shape, but because he'd needed to be lower, needed gravity to be less of a variable, needed to exist in a space small enough that the enormity of *Taipei* and *alive* and *requesting transport* couldn't quite reach him. Sera had sat with him the entire time, the Bond carrying her quiet certainty like a heartbeat, her presence the one fixed point in a world that had just tilted on its axis.

Lily Vale was Sera's sister — nineteen years old, dark-haired, sharp-eyed, with the Vale family's tendency toward competence and none of the Stage 4 complications. She'd been running New Eden's logistics since her rescue from the Vale Industries panic room: supply management, personnel allocation, the unglamorous work of keeping a settlement functional. She had her mother's head for systems and her father's stubbornness, and she'd been crying recently, though she'd never admit it.
"Vivienne told me," she said, standing in the radio room doorway. Her voice was careful — not pitying, not clinical, just *present*. "About the broadcast. About your parents."
Marcus wiped his face with his sleeve. "Yeah."

Lily nodded. She had a tablet tucked under her arm — her own, salvaged from the Vale Industries server room, loaded with building schematics and inventory data and the detritus of a corporate empire that no longer existed. She pulled it out now and tapped through screens with the rapid efficiency of someone who'd been doing this since she was twelve.

"Vale Industries Tower has a satellite uplink on the roof," she said. "Commercial grade — HughesNet JUPITER system, installed for executive video conferencing before the outbreak. The dish is intact. I checked it myself during the siege." She looked up from the tablet. "If I can get it working, we can establish two-way communication with any satellite-connected relay in the world. Including Taipei."
Marcus stared at her. "You're telling me there's a working satellite uplink—"

"Potentially working. The hardware is there. The power supply needs to be rerouted — the tower's generator is separate from New Eden's, and I don't know if it's still operational. The firmware will need updating — it's two years out of date, and the constellation configuration has probably changed since the outbreak." Lily's eyes were bright with the particular light of a Vale woman confronting a solvable problem. "But yes. If I can get it working, we can *talk* to them. Not just listen. Two-way communication. Real-time."

Through the Bond, Marcus felt Sera's reaction — a complicated weave of hope and protectiveness and the fierce, defiant optimism of a creature who'd been told she was a monster and had chosen to be a sister instead. Lily was offering something impossible. Lily was offering *clarity* — the chance to hear his parents' voices, to know they were real and not a radio illusion, to tell them he was alive too.
"How long?" Marcus asked.

"To get the uplink working?" Lily considered. "A week if everything goes right. Two weeks if it doesn't. Longer if the tower's generator is completely fried." She looked at Sera, then back at Marcus. "But I can do it. I want to do it." A pause. "You gave me my sister back. Let me give you your parents."
The System pulsed.
**[NEW QUEST CHAIN INITIATED: The Family Reunion]**

**[QUEST 1: Establish Satellite Communication with Taipei]**
**[DESCRIPTION: The shortwave radio proved they're alive. A satellite uplink can prove you are too. Repair the Vale Industries Tower satellite system and establish two-way real-time communication with the Taipei survivor network.]**
**[ESTIMATED DIFFICULTY: EXTREME]**
**[REQUIREMENTS: Restore tower generator, repair/replace satellite dish components, update firmware, align with operational satellite constellation]**
**[TIME LIMIT: None — but David and Mei-Ling Chen are requesting transport. Every day of delay is a day they wait alone.]**

**[QUEST 2: Rescue David and Mei-Ling Chen]**
**[DESCRIPTION: Your parents survived the outbreak. They are sheltering in Taipei, which maintains partial infrastructure and military protection. But "requesting transport" means they can't get to you. You need to get to them — or arrange extraction from 8,000 miles away in a world where international travel has ceased to exist.]**
**[ESTIMATED DIFFICULTY: EXTREME]**
**[REQUIREMENTS: Unknown — no existing infrastructure for trans-Pacific travel. Options may include: military convoy, maritime vessel, or creative solutions not yet conceived.]**

**[QUEST CHAIN REWARD: Unknown]**
**[NOTE: Family is the first bond. The System was built by a father who lost his son. It would be a poor tool indeed if it could not help a son find his parents.]**

Marcus read the notification twice. The last line — *a father who lost his son* — landed in his chest like a fist. Richard Vale. The System's creator. Sera and Lily's father. A man who had built a machine to heal loneliness because he'd been unable to heal his own, and who had embedded in its code something that looked, from this angle, astonishingly like love.
He stood up. Sera's hand fell from his neck. Lily lowered the tablet.
"When do we start?" he asked.
Lily almost smiled. "I've already started. I need three people and a wiring diagram." She looked at Sera. "And possibly a zombie escort to the tower. The building isn't fully cleared."

"You'll have one," Sera said. Her voice carried the quiet authority of a queen — not the Zombie Queen of rumors and terror, but the queen of New Eden, a woman who had found her kingdom not by conquering it but by choosing to protect it. "Whatever you need."

Marcus looked out the window. The sunset was fading — the same amber-to-indigo gradient he'd watched a hundred times from a hundred different windows, in a basement and a penthouse and a titanium-walled fortress that had become a home. Somewhere beyond that horizon, eight thousand miles west and a lifetime away, David and Mei-Ling Chen were waiting for their son.
And their son was coming.

"Yeah," he said, and his voice was steady now, the tremor gone, the grief and the shock transmuted by the Bond and the promise into something harder, something that could survive the journey ahead. "Yeah, we'll find them."
Through the Bond, Sera's response was wordless and absolute: *You won't do it alone.*
Through the open door, Vivienne's voice drifted from the shortwave, still cycling, still broadcasting, still reminding a dead world that the dead weren't everyone:
*"The Relay, broadcasting on 14.250 MHz. If you're receiving this transmission, you are not alone..."*

Not alone. Never alone. That was the System's promise, and the Bond's proof, and the reason — the only reason that mattered — that a man who'd spent three years hiding from the world was now planning to cross an ocean to find the people who'd taught him what love was before the world ended.
The radio crackled. The satellite waited. The sea stretched between New Eden and Taipei like a question mark.
Marcus Chen was going to answer it.
